ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for water spinach as an ingredient (FOODON_03311737); no exact FoodOn code exists for the prepared stir-fried sambal dish. Adapted from the closest ingredient-level anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Swamp cabbage / water spinach — cooked vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers swamp cabbage / water spinach as a food commodity. Applied at dish level as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; no specific code exists for the sambal-dressed stir-fried preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried kang kong with sambal as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried kang kong with sambal weighs approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve vegetable side dish portion at a Singaporean hawker centre or casual restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo / HPB Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for '601D Stir-fried Kang Kong with Sambal' was found in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore food composition data for stir-fried water spinach with sambal / belacan, cross-referenced with the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input values are retained where consistent with HPB reference ranges for comparable stir-fried leafy vegetable dishes. Energy recalculated: protein 2.4 g × 4 + carbohydrate 7.2 g × 4 + fat 8.0 g × 9 = 9.6 + 28.8 + 72.0 = 110.4 kcal ≈ 112 kcal (within rounding tolerance). Values accepted as plausible.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — leafy green vegetables and mixed vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kang kong with sambal. GI estimated at 32 (low) by analogy with cooked leafy green vegetables and mixed stir-fried vegetable dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Water spinach is a low-GI leafy vegetable; sambal paste contributes modest sugar but does not materially raise the overall GI of the dish. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 18.0) / 100 = 5.76 ≈ 5.8 (serving 250 g, carbohydrate 7.2 g/100 g × 2.5 = 18.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100 g from belacan, fish sauce, and added salt; partially offset by potassium ~280 mg/100 g from kang kong
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced belacan and fish sauce and ask for no added salt, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit — though the meaningful potassium content from kang kong provides partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~32), modest carbohydrate 7.2 g/100 g, and dietary fibre 1.6 g/100 g moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kang kong with sambal is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic index and modest carbohydrate load (18 g per 250 g serving), but sambal paste contains added sugar and individuals should request reduced sambal to minimise glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (380 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 1.2 g/100 g and trans fat is absent
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) and absence of trans fat make this dish broadly heart-friendly, but the high sodium content from sambal condiments poses a cardiovascular risk and individuals with CVD should request reduced belacan and fish sauce to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant-rich leafy green base (vitamin C 10 mg/100 g) with anti-inflammatory polyphenols from chilli and garlic; low saturated fat and zero trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kang kong provides antioxidants and fibre that may support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns in rheumatoid arthritis, but individuals sensitive to nightshade solanine (from chilli) should monitor their response, and belacan-derived sodium should be minimised to reduce RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kang Kong with Sambal (叁巴炒空心菜; Malay: Kangkung Goreng Sambal) is a popular Singaporean hawker and home-cooked vegetable side dish featuring water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with a spicy sambal sauce made from ground chilli, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), shallots, garlic, and a touch of sugar and fish sauce. The dish delivers a vibrant combination of smoky wok-hei aroma, tender-crisp stems, wilted leafy greens, and a bold, savoury-spicy sambal coating. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 112 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 8.0 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil, with low saturated fat at 1.2 g), 7.2 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g sugar, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 380 mg sodium. The dish has a low glycaemic index (~32) making it suitable for blood glucose management, though its sodium content from belacan and fish sauce warrants caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Kang kong is a rich source of vitamin C, beta-carotene, and iron, and the chilli and garlic in sambal contribute polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties. A standard serving is one plate (~250 g). The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains crustacean allergens (belacan) and fish allergens (fish sauce), and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is commonly served as a vegetable accompaniment to rice at Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and Chinese-style restaurants.
common_user_queries: Is stir-fried kang kong with sambal healthy?
How many calories in kangkung goreng sambal?
Is kang kong sambal suitable for diabetics?
Is kangkong belacan high in sodium?
Can I eat kang kong sambal if I have high blood pressure?
What are the nutrients in stir-fried water spinach?
How much sodium in kang kong sambal?

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific dish was found in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore reference data for comparable stir-fried leafy vegetable dishes with sambal, cross-referenced with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Energy calculation is internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference data for cooked leafy green vegetables. Potassium value (280 mg/100 g) is estimated from HPB water spinach composition data and is not directly measured for this specific preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for stir-fried kang kong with sambal is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific stir-fried sambal preparation in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in vegetable oil-based stir-fry preparations per HPB reference data. potassium_mg value of 280 mg/100 g is estimated from HPB water spinach raw composition data adjusted for cooking losses; not directly measured for this sambal preparation. Input record cuisine was listed as 'Thai' but the dish is a Singaporean hawker staple of Southeast Asian origin; cuisine corrected to 'Singaporean' with Thai-script alias retained given the Thai culinary influence on sambal kangkong preparations. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical '1 plate / 250 g' entries) collapsed to single serving record. Halal status marked false as belacan (shrimp paste) authenticity and sourcing cannot be confirmed without vendor certification.
